author: Vitor
explanation: This code finds the best matching word in `wordsContainer` for each search word in `wordsQuery` by looking for the longest shared ending (suffix). It does this efficiently by building a search tree (called a Trie) and inserting the container words backward, from right to left. As it builds the tree, each node keeps track of the "best" word index that shares that suffix, prioritizing shorter words and using the earliest-appearing word as a tiebreaker. Finally, for each query, the code traverses the tree backward to find the longest matching suffix path possible and retrieves its stored index; if no characters match at all, it automatically falls back to the index of the overall shortest and earliest word in the container.

author: Vitor
explanation: This code finds the most similar word in a list (`wordsContainer`) for each search word in a query (`wordsQuery`) by matching their endings (suffixes). To do this efficiently, it builds a tree-like data structure called a Trie, inserting all the container words in reverse order (from last character to first). At every node in this tree, the code dynamically keeps track of the "best" word index that shares that specific suffix, prioritizing the shortest word overall and using the earliest appearing word to break any ties. Finally, for each query word, the code traverses this tree backwards to find the longest matching suffix possible, immediately returning the pre-calculated best index stored at the furthest matching point.

author: Vitor
explanation: This code finds the index of the word in `wordsContainer` that shares the longest common suffix (ending) with each query word in `wordsQuery`, prioritizing the shortest word (and the earliest one in the list in case of a tie). To do this efficiently, it first builds a search tree (a Trie) by inserting all container words backwards, character by character. As it builds the tree, each node keeps track of the index of the shortest word that shares that suffix so far. Finally, for each query word, the code traverses this tree backwards to find the longest matching suffix path and immediately retrieves the pre-saved index of the shortest, best-matching word.

author: Vitor
explanation: This code finds the best matching word from a list (`wordsContainer`) for each search word in a query list (`wordsQuery`) based on how much of their endings (suffixes) they share, prioritizing the shortest word in case of a tie. To do this efficiently, it reverses all the words and inserts them into a "Trie" (a tree-like data structure used for quick character searches). While building this tree, each node automatically remembers the index of the shortest word that has passed through it so far. When a query word is reversed and searched in the tree, the code follows the matching characters as far as possible and instantly returns the index of the shortest word sharing the longest common suffix.
